coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of phosphate ion in environmental water samples using flavin mononucleotide-functionalized Fe3O4 particles

coredata.dc:description: Phosphate ion (PO4 3−) serves as an important nutrient carrier to support the growth of aquatic animals and plants in aquatic systems. However, excess concentrations of PO4 3− are the key factor responsible for eutrophication, resulting in rapid deterioration of water quality. Therefore, accurate determination of PO4 3− is of great significance in water quality and security. In this study, flavin mononucleotide (FMN), an intracellular form of vitamin B2, was used as fluorophore. A novel “off-on” fluorescent sensing platform (FMN@Fe3O4) was fabricated for selective and sensitive detection of PO4 3−, and showed excellent fluorescence response and good selectivity for PO4 3− detection. With the addition of PO4 3−, the fluorescence intensity restored is proportional to PO4 3− concentration in the quantification range of 50 nM–0.75 μM with a limit of detection as low as 20 nM (0.62 μg.L−1, calculated by P element). An adsorption/desorption sensing mechanism via an in-depth analysis of the interfacial interaction between PO4 3− and FMN@Fe3O4 is proposed. FMN is first adsorbed by its terminal phosphate group on Fe3O4 particles to quench fluorescence. Free PO4 3− replaces the adsorbed FMN and restores the quenched fluorescence to achieve the aim of PO4 3− detection. In addition, this sensing system has been successfully validated in real water sample analysis and all reagents involved are nontoxic, environmentally benign, and easily-available. Therefore, this assay has great applicability in water quality monitoring.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of phosphate ion (PO4 3−) detection using a FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe. The detection process is comprised of two steps: FMN adsorption (“Turn off” Fluorescence) onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles through its terminal phosphate group and desorption (“Turn on” Fluorescence) through replacement by free PO4 3−.

(a) SEM image of the Fe3O4 particles. The inset shows dynamic light scattering (DLS) particle size analysis of the Fe3O4 particles. (b) XRD pattern analysis of the Fe3O4 particles. (c) High-resolution TEM image of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe and the corresponding elemental mappings of Fe, O, C, N and P. (d) FTIR spectra of Fe3O4, FMN and the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy characterization. (a) survey of the FMN@Fe3O4 probe, (b) high-resolution spectrum of Fe (2p), (c) high-resolution spectrum of O (1 s), (d) high-resolution spectrum of C (1 s), (e) high-resolution spectrum of N (1 s), and (f) high-resolution spectrum of P (2p).

(a) Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectrum of free FMN solution (500 nM). (b) UV–vis adsorption spectrum (100 μM) and fluorescence emission spectrum (500 nM) of free FMN solutions. (c) Fluorescence spectra of free FMN, FMN@Fe3O4, and FMN@Fe3O4 in the presence of PO4 3−. (d) Fluorescence photographs demonstrating FMN adsorption (Fluorescence “OFF”) onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles through its terminal phosphate group, and desorption (Fluorescence “ON”) through replacement by free PO4 3−. The initial concentrations of FMN, Fe3O4 particles, and PO4 3− were 500 nM, 100 μg.mL−1 and 100 μM, respectively.

(a) Effect of initial pHs on the fluorescence intensities of free FMN solutions. The initial concentration of free FMN solution was 500 nM. The initial pHs of the FMN solutions were pre-adjusted to pH (1.0–13.0) with HCl (1.0 M) and NaOH (1.0 M). An acetate buffer solution (pH = 5.8, 94 mM NaAc and 6 mM HAc) was also used as pH control. (b) Effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensities of free FMN solutions. (c) Fluorescence quenching of free FMN solutions incubated with different concentration of Fe3O4 particles. (d) Variation in fluorescence intensities of FMN solutions at 523 nm in response to different concentrations of Fe3O4 particles. The initial concentration of FMN solution was 500 nM. The initial concentration of Fe3O4 particles was in the range of 20–800 μg.mL−1.

(a) The adsorption of FMN (blue) onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles and desorption of FMN (red) from Fe3O4 particles replaced by PO4 3−. The initial concentrations of FMN and Fe3O4 particles were 500 nM and 100 μg.mL−1, respectively. The PO4 3−concentration added was 100 μM. (b) The adsorption isotherm of FMN molecules (500 nM) on the surface of Fe3O4 particles (100 μg.mL−1). The inset shows a linear relationship at low FMN equilibrium concentrations (0–400 nM).

(a) Selectivity of PO4 3− analysis using this FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe against other anions (10 μM) without PO4 3−. The inset shows the fluorescence spectra of FMN@Fe3O4 solutions in the presence of different anions. (b) Response of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe to other anions (10 μM) in the presence of PO4 3− (1.0 mM). The inset shows the fluorescence spectra of FMN@Fe3O4 suspension solutions in the presence of different anions co-existed with PO4 3− (1.0 mM). The initial concentration of FMN@Fe3O4 suspension solutions was 100 μg.mL−1.

(a) Fluorescence recovery of FMN@Fe3O4 suspension solutions (100 μg.mL−1) in response to different concentrations of PO4 3− (0.05–7.5 μM). The adsorption peak at 445 nm was selected as excitation wavelength. The emission peak at 523 nm was selected to monitor the fluorescence recovery of FMN@Fe3O4 in response to different concentrations of PO4 3−. (b) Variation in fluorescence intensities of FMN@Fe3O4 suspension solutions at 523 nm in response to different concentrations of PO4 3− (0.05–7.5 μM). Inset: amplification of the calibration curve of the low concentration range of PO4 3− (0.05–0.75 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of riboflavin (RF) solutions in the presence of both Fe3O4 particles and PO4 3−. The initial concentrations of RF, Fe3O4 particles, and PO4 3− were 500 nM, 100 μg.mL−1 and 100 μM, respectively. (b) Zeta potentials variation of Fe3O4 particles in different solutions. The initial concentrations of FMN (RF) and Fe3O4 particles were 500 nM and 100 μg.mL−1, respectively. The PO4 3−concentration added was 100 μM. (c) Schematic representation of sensing PO4 3− using this FMN@Fe3O4 as fluorescent probe.
Determination of phosphate ions (PO4 3−) in tap water and lake water samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphate ion (PO4 3−) serves as an important nutrient carrier to support the growth of aquatic animals and plants in aquatic systems. However, excess concentrations of PO4 3− are the key factor responsible for eutrophication, resulting in rapid deterioration of water quality. Therefore, accurate determination of PO4 3− is of great significance in water quality and security. In this study, flavin mononucleotide (FMN), an intracellular form of vitamin B2, was used as fluorophore. A novel “off-on” fluorescent sensing platform (FMN@Fe3O4) was fabricated for selective and sensitive detection of PO4 3−, and showed excellent fluorescence response and good selectivity for PO4 3− detection. With the addition of PO4 3−, the fluorescence intensity restored is proportional to PO4 3− concentration in the quantification range of 50 nM–0.75 μM with a limit of detection as low as 20 nM (0.62 μg.L−1, calculated by P element). An adsorption/desorption sensing mechanism via an in-depth analysis of the interfacial interaction between PO4 3− and FMN@Fe3O4 is proposed. FMN is first adsorbed by its terminal phosphate group on Fe3O4 particles to quench fluorescence. Free PO4 3− replaces the adsorbed FMN and restores the quenched fluorescence to achieve the aim of PO4 3− detection. In addition, this sensing system has been successfully validated in real water sample analysis and all reagents involved are nontoxic, environmentally benign, and easily-available. Therefore, this assay has great applicability in water quality monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Phosphate ion (PO4 3−), the dominant species of phosphorus, plays a central role in a wide range of biological and chemical processes in both aquatic ecosystems and biological systems (Conley et al., 2009; Wild et al., 2016). PO4 3− serves as a growth-limiting nutrient to support the growth of aquatic animals and plants in aquatic systems (Sattari et al., 2012). However, excess concentrations of PO4 3− are the key factor responsible for eutrophication resulting in rapid deterioration of water quality (Lewis et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2017; Clinton-Bailey et al., 2017). In biological systems, the concentration of PO4 3− in bodily fluids is causal to diverse physiological functions, such as physiological pH balance, signal transduction and energy transfer (Chande and Bergwitz, 2018; Ma et al., 2020). PO4 3− deficiency has been reported to cause some clinical consequences, such as hypophosphatemia (Haffner et al., 2019). Meanwhile, excessive PO4 3− has been implicated in some renal diseases, and long-term exposure to high-concentration PO4 3−-containing drinking water has been responsible for causing damage to human digestion systems (Ma et al., 2020). Therefore, accurate determination of PO4 3−concentration is of great significance for water quality and security.

To date, the standard method of PO4 3− determination is still phosphomolybdenum blue (PMB)-based spectrophotometry, which was first reported in 1962 (Murphy and Riley, 1962). However, this classic colorimetric technique requires continued consumption of chemical reagents, including ammonium molybdate, ascorbic acid and antimony (III), which produces toxic waste and greatly limits its field applications (Chen et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020). Recently, great efforts have been devoted to exploring novel methods for PO4 3− determination in practical applications, such as colorimetric assay (Mak et al., 2003; Kolliopoulos et al., 2015; Cinti et al., 2016; Guan et al., 2021), ion chromatography (Quintana et al., 2006; Yokoyama et al., 2009; Yang et al., 2014), electrochemical assay (Rahman et al., 2006; Berchmans et al., 2012; Warwick et al., 2013; Kolliopoulos et al., 2015; Cinti et al., 2016), biosensors (Mak et al., 2003; Rahman et al., 2006; Basheer et al., 2011) and fluorescence-based methods (Dai et al., 2015; Cao et al., 2015; Wu and Tong, 2018). However, these strategies often suffer from time-consuming, sophisticated-instruments-dependent and not applicable in field studies (Warwick et al., 2013; Alam et al., 2021). Thus, there is still strong demand for novel methods of sensitive and selective determination of PO4 3− in water quality and security.

Among potential successors to PMB-based spectrophotometry, fluorescence-based technologies have aroused wide research interest, given their potential to overcome the above-mentioned disadvantages of existing methods (Dai et al., 2015; Zou et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2020). Various kinds of fluorescence probes have been designed for the detection of PO4 3−, due to their excellent properties of high sensitivity, simplicity and specificity (Peng et al., 2007; Vegesna et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2017; Wu and Tong, 2018). However, most of these probes rely on organic fluorescence molecules, which often suffer from complicated-synthesis, cytotoxicity, poor water-solubility and easy aggregation in aqueous solutions (Ma et al., 2020). Thus, the discovery of nontoxic, water-soluble, environmentally benign, and easily-available fluorescence molecules for the analysis of PO4 3− in aqueous matrices is highly desired.

Flavin mononucleotide (FMN, an intracellular form of vitamin B2) and its derivatives are widely present in many enzymes as important cofactors and play vital roles in many electron transfer processes (Shi et al., 2012; Orita et al., 2016; Ravikumar et al., 2017; Savelyev et al., 2017). FMN has a unique planar isoalloxazine ring, a polyhydroxy side chain with a terminal phosphate group (Fig. 1 ), and shows excellent inherent fluorescence properties. In the field of environmental engineering, iron oxides have been extensively used in the removal of phosphorus in wastewater treatment (Martin et al., 2009; Wu et al., 2017; Salehin et al., 2020), indicating that there is a high affinity between PO4 3− and iron oxides (Nowack and Stone, 2006; Liu and Liu, 2014; Reinhardt et al., 2020).This connection between the terminal phosphate group of FMN and iron oxides inspired us to examine the possibility of the detection of PO4 3− using FMN as fluorophore.

In this study, we demonstrate a novel fluorescence sensing system (FMN@Fe3O4) for selective and sensitive detection of PO4 3−, which employs FMN as fluorophore. FMN has a polyhydroxy side chain with a terminal phosphate group (Shi et al., 2012; Orita et al., 2016), which means that FMN can be adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. The terminal phosphate group of FMN shares similar chemical characteristics with free PO4 3−, and can be replaced by free PO4 3− in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 1, the FMN molecule is first adsorbed by its terminal phosphate group on Fe3O4 particles to quench fluorescence (the “Turn Off” stage). Then, free PO4 3− in aqueous samples replaces the adsorbed FMN and restores the quenched fluorescence (the “Turn On” stage). Using this strategy, we designed a novel adsorption/desorption fluorescence sensing system (FMN@Fe3O4), using FMN as fluorophore for the selective and sensitive detection of PO4 3−, which is of great importance for environmental water quality monitoring.

FMN and riboflavin (RF) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium acetate (NaAC), acetic acid (HAC), sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium iodide (NaI), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ferrous sulfate, heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O), and ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., (Shanghai, China) without further purification. All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification. All solutions were prepared in ultrapure water prepared using a Milli-Q ultrapure water system (Milli-Q Inc., USA).

UV–vis absorbance spectra of FMN and RF were obtained using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1.0-cm standard quartz cell. Morphological analysis of Fe3O4 particles and FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe were performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; SU8020, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). Chemical mapping of Fe3O4 particles and FMN@Fe3O4 was further performed through energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. The particle sizes and zeta-potentials were measured using a Nano ZS zetasizer (Malvern Instrument, UK). The crystalline structure analysis of Fe3O4 particles was conducted using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD; Bruker, Germany) with CuKa radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). FTIR spectra were recorded from 4000 to 400 cm−1 using an IS-10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo, USA) operated at a setting of 32 scans at a spectral resolution of 4.0 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron analysis was performed on an ESCALAB250 system (XPS, Thermo-VG Scientific Inc., UK) using C (1 s) peak at 284.6 eV as binding energy reference.

All fluorescence analysis was carried out on an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) fluorescence analysis (excitation wavelength: 300–550 nm, emission wavelength: 400–700 nm) and two-dimensional fluorescence analysis (excitation wavelength: 445 nm, emission wavelength: 450–700 nm) of free FMN solutions were conducted to reveal the FMN fluorescence properties. The slit widths were set as 5.0 nm for both the excitation and emission monochromators. Fluorescence intensity of the emission peak at 523 nm was used to monitor the efficiency of the adsorption and desorption processes of FMN molecules on the surface of Fe3O4 particles.

Magnetic Fe3O4 particles used in this work were first prepared using a hydrothermal co-precipitation method (Liang et al., 2006). The FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe used in this work was then prepared through incubating as-prepared magnetic Fe3O4 particles with FMN molecules (acetate buffer, pH = 5.8). Free FMN solution was first incubated with Fe3O4 to reach adsorption equilibrium. The initial concentration of free FMN solution was 500 nM. The initial concentration of Fe3O4 was 100 μg.mL−1. The adsorption process of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 was monitored using fluorescence spectroscopy. Strong fluorescence quenching of the FMN solution after the addition of Fe3O4 particles was used to confirm that FMN had been successfully adsorbed onto the surface of the Fe3O4. The as-prepared FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probes were magnetically separated to remove unadsorbed FMN molecules, and then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The effect of initial pH values of the free FMN solutions on the fluorescence intensity was firstly evaluated. FMN solution was prepared in ultrapure water to a final concentration of 500 nM. Initial pH values of the FMN solutions were pre-adjusted to different pHs (1.0–13.0) with HCl solution (1.0 M) and NaOH solution (1.0 M). The variation in fluorescence intensities of the free FMN molecules under different pHs was recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer. The effect of electrolytes on the fluorescence intensity of FMN was further tested under similar conditions, using NaCl as a model electrolyte. Variation of fluorescence intensity of FMN solutions incubated with different concentrations of electrolyte (NaCl, 0–400 mM) was monitored using the fluorescence method.

In order to reveal the response kinetics of the adsorption process, FMN solution (500 nM) was first incubated with Fe3O4 particles (100 μg.mL−1) to reach adsorption equilibrium. The adsorption curve of FMN molecules on the surface of Fe3O4 particles was measured through monitoring the variation in fluorescence intensities of the FMN solutions, and the adsorption amount of FMN was further calculated. After reaching the adsorption equilibrium, the fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 523 nm was measured to study the desorption process of FMN in the presence of PO4 3− under ambient temperature (25 °C).

To investigate the detection ability of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe toward PO4 3−, FMN@Fe3O4 suspensions (100 μg.mL−1) were incubated with different concentrations of PO4 3− (0.05 μM-7.50 mM). After 60-min incubation under ambient temperature (25 °C), the fluorescence recovery of the FMN@Fe3O4 suspensions was measured. Selectivity of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe for PO4 3− analysis against other anions (10 μM) was further evaluated. The interference effect of co-existing ions (10 μM) to PO4 3− detection was also explored in the presence of PO4 3− (10 μM). Fluorescence analysis of the above FMN@Fe3O4 suspensions with or without PO4 3− (10 μM) were performed after 60-min incubation under ambient temperature (25 °C). All experimental data presented in the results are the averaged values of five parallel analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in real environmental samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Typical real environmental water samples were collected and analyzed in this study, including tap water, lake water, and municipal wastewater samples. Tap water samples were collected at a lab located in Hefei University of Technology (Hefei, China). Lake water samples were collected from Chaohu Lake (Hefei, China). Municipal wastewater samples were collected from five individual sampling points (1. Influent; 2. Aerobic tank; 3. Anaerobic tank; 4. Sedimentation tank; and 5. Effluent.) in a municipal sewage treatment plant located in Hefei, China. Prior to fluorescence analysis, all water samples were centrifuged to remove mixed liquid suspended solids and filtered with a SPE cartridge (Sep-pak C18, Waters) to eliminate the influence of dissolved organic matters. High-concentration water samples were further diluted ten-fold with ultrapure water before fluorescence analysis.

FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe (100 μg.mL−1) was added into the real water samples and incubated under ambient temperature (25 °C) for 60 min, and then fluorescence restoration was recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer. The adsorption peak at 445 nm was selected as excitation wavelength. The emission peak at 523 nm was selected to monitor the fluorescence recovery of FMN@Fe3O4 in response to different concentrations of PO4 3−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: To test our hypothesis, highly dispersed Fe3O4 particles were first prepared using a hydrothermal co-precipitation method (Liang et al., 2006). Both SEM imaging (Fig. 2a) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) particle size analysis (inset of Fig. 2a) illustrate that as-prepared Fe3O4 particles have good particle size distribution with an average size of 285 nm. The XRD pattern analysis was employed to confirm the chemical structure of as-prepared Fe3O4 particles. As shown in Fig. 2b, XRD patterns demonstrated a series of characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ values 31.25°, 36.82°, 44.76°, 47.50°, 57.20°and 65.19°, which can be ascribed to typical planes of cubic phase of Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 19-0629): (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (5 5 1) and (4 4 0). All these observed characteristic diffraction peaks further confirmed the formation of Fe3O4 particles (Liang et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2021).

FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was then prepared through mixing Fe3O4 particles (100 μg.mL−1) with FMN aqueous solution (500 nM, acetate buffer, pH = 5.8). The as-prepared FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was magnetically separated to remove free FMN molecules. High-resolution TEM images further confirmed the size distributions of Fe3O4 particles (Fig. S1) and FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe (Fig. 2c) obtained by SEM and DLS analysis. EDS mappings of Fe and O further confirmed the elemental composition of the Fe3O4 particles (Fig. S2). The distribution of elements C, N, and P was uniform on the surface of the Fe3O4 particles, verifying the adsorption of FMN onto the surface of the Fe3O4 particles (Fig. 2c).

FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was then prepared through mixing Fe3O4 particles (100 μg.mL−1) with FMN aqueous solution (500 nM, acetate buffer, pH = 5.8). The as-prepared FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was magnetically separated to remove free FMN molecules. High-resolution TEM images further confirmed the size distributions of Fe3O4 particles (Fig. S1) and FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe (Fig. 2c) obtained by SEM and DLS analysis. EDS mappings of Fe and O further confirmed the elemental composition of the Fe3O4 particles (Fig. S2). The distribution of elements C, N, and P was uniform on the surface of the Fe3O4 particles, verifying the adsorption of FMN onto the surface of the Fe3O4 particles (Fig. 2c).

The chemical composition of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was further investigated using FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 2d shows the FTIR spectra of Fe3O4, FMN and FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe. A quick survey of the FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4 reveals that there was a strong absorbance band at 598 cm−1, which can be assigned to FeO stretching vibration. In the FTIR spectrum of FMN, there are four characteristic absorbance bands at 3500–3000 cm−1, 1725–1650 cm−1, 1575–1535 cm−1 and 1115–995 cm−1. The strong absorbance band at 3500–3000 cm−1 can be assigned to O-H/N-H stretching vibrations. The strong absorbance bands at 1725–1650 cm−1 can be assigned to CO stretching vibration. The characteristic absorbance bands at 1575–1535 cm−1 can be assigned to C=C/C=N stretching vibrations. The strong wide absorbance band at 1115–995 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of PO4 3−. The obvious absorbance bands at 1725–1650 cm−1, 1575–1535 cm−1, and 1115–995 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of FMN@Fe3O4 indicate the successful adsorption of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. The wide absorbance bands at 1115–995 cm−1 were characteristic vibrations of the terminal phosphate group in FMN molecule. After the adsorption on the surface of Fe3O4 particles, the symmetry of this characteristic asymmetric stretch vibration of FMN decrease obviously, which indicates the replacement of the surface hydroxyl groups by terminal phosphate group with the formation of inner-sphere complex (Paltrinieri et al., 2017; Almasri et al., 2019). The adsorption of FMN onto the surface of the Fe3O4 particles was further confirmed by measuring the ζ-potentials of Fe3O4 suspensions with and without the presence of FMN molecules. The ζ-potential of bare Fe3O4 particles was measured as −38.2 ± 0.3 mV. When FMN molecules were added, the ζ-potential shifted to −47.5 ± 0.5 mV, further confirming the formation of FMN@Fe3O4.

The surface chemistry of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was investigated using XPS analysis. As shown in the survey spectrum of FMN@Fe3O4 (Fig. 3a), the characteristic peaks can be attributed to Fe, O, C, N, and P elements. In the high-resolution spectra of Fe (2p) (Fig. 3b), there are two characteristic peaks for Fe(2P1/2) at 710.9 eV and Fe(2P3/2) at 724.6 eV. The Fe(2P1/2) peak at 710.9 eV can be further deconvoluted into two distinct components, which were attributed to Fe2+ at 710.3 eV and Fe3+ at 712.7 eV (Xu et al., 2021). The Fe (2P3/2) peak at 724.6 eV can also be viewed as two characteristic peaks. The characteristic peak at 724.3 eV attributed to the Fe2+ component of Fe3O4 and the characteristic peak at 724.8 eV attributed to the Fe3+ component of Fe3O4, respectively. The O (1 s) spectrum of FMN@Fe3O4 (Fig. 3c) can be further deconvoluted into three distinct components at 529.7, 530.4, and 531.6 eV, which can be attributed to the CO covalent bonds contained in FMN molecules, the FeO chemical bonds contained in Fe3O4 particles, and the chemisorbed oxygen/water on the surface of Fe3O4 particles, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3d, four distinct components can be deconvoluted in the high resolution spectrum of C (1 s) peak of FMN@Fe3O4, which correspond to four kinds of carbon atoms in different chemical environment: CC (284.4 eV, 284.9 eV), CO (285.8 eV), and CN (288.1 eV) (Xu et al., 2021). Two characteristic peaks of N (1 s), located at 399.9 eV (Fig. 3e), and P (2p) located at 133.3 eV (Fig. 3f), can be attributed to the elements of N and P contained in FMN molecules, which further indicates the adsorption of FMN onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles. Based on the analysis above, the formation of FMN@Fe3O4 was further confirmed through XPS analysis, which are consistent with the experimental results from TEM, XRD, and FTIR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic characterization of FMN and FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: FMN has a unique planar isoalloxazine ring endowed with excellent inherent fluorescence properties. 3D-EEM fluorescence analysis was first performed to reveal the fluorescence properties of FMN. The excitation wavelength was set at 300–550 nm, and the emission wavelength was set at 400–700 nm. There were two main emission peaks, at 375/523 nm and 445/523 nm in the 3D-EEM fluorescence spectrum of the FMN solution, as shown in Fig. 4a. UV–vis absorbance analysis was further performed. There are four strong absorbance peaks at 222, 266, 375 and 445 nm (blue line, Fig. 4b). To avoid possible damage to the FMN molecules under UV excitation, the absorbance peak of 445 nm was selected and used as the excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence emission at 523 nm (red line, Fig. 4b) was used to monitor the adsorption/desorption processes of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4. Given the interfacial interaction between the FMN molecules and Fe3O4 particles, adsorption/desorption of FMN with Fe3O4 particles was performed to confirm the feasibility of the detection of PO4 3− using FMN as a fluorescent molecule. The variation of fluorescence intensity was monitored during the adsorption of free FMN molecules on the surface of Fe3O4 and the replacement of adsorbed FMN by free PO4 3−. As shown in Fig. 4c, the incubation of free FMN molecules with Fe3O4 particles result in an obvious fluorescence quenching, indicating the formation of FMN@Fe3O4. The addition of free PO4 3− ions restores the fluorescence of FMN. Fluorescence photographs (Fig. 4d) demonstrate FMN adsorption (Fluorescence “OFF”) on the surface of Fe3O4 particles through its terminal phosphate group and desorption (Fluorescence “ON”) through replacement by free PO4 3−.

Given that the fluorescence emission process is usually pH-sensitive and that the addition of PO4 3− may induce a pH change to the FMN solution, the effect of pH on the fluorescence of FMN solutions was systematically evaluated. The initial pHs of FMN solutions was then adjusted and it was found that the fluorescence intensity of FMN decreased drastically at both high and low pH values (Fig. 5a). Thus, acetate buffer solution (pH = 5.8, 94 mM NaAc and 6 mM HAc) was used as a pH control.

Both bare Fe3O4 particles, with a ζ-potential of −38.2 ± 0.3 mV, and the terminal phosphate group of FMN are negatively charged. There should be a strong electrostatic repulsion interaction (Liu and Liu, 2014), which is unbeneficial to the adsorption of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4. Thus, NaCl was employed as an electrolyte to eliminate this interaction. The effect of NaCl concentration onto the adsorption of FMN onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles was systematically evaluated by monitoring the variations in fluorescence intensities of the FMN solutions. The addition of NaCl to free FMN solutions slightly decreased fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5b), and a 100 mM concentration of NaCl was used in the subsequent experiments. The influence of Fe3O4 dosage on the adsorption curve of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 was further evaluated. The initial concentration of FMN solution was 500 nM. The initial concentration of Fe3O4 particles was in the range of 20–800 μg.mL−1. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence quenching of the FMN solutions was initiated with the addition of Fe3O4 particles. The fluorescence intensity of the FMN solutions decreased from 600 a.u. to 200 a.u., with concentrations of Fe3O4 particles increasing from 20 μg.mL−1 to 500 μg.mL−1. Higher concentrations of Fe3O4 particles led to a drastic decrease of fluorescence intensity as more FMN molecules were absorbed onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles. However, further increase of Fe3O4 concentration did not result in further decreases in fluorescence intensity of the FMN solutions, which maintained a relatively constant value (Fig. 5d). Thus, the concentration of Fe3O4 particles was set at 100 μg.mL−1 to ensure complete coverage of FMN molecules on the surface of Fe3O4 particles, while the initial concentration of FMN solution was set at 500 nM.

Fluorescence emission at 523 nm under excitation of 445 nm was selected to monitor the adsorption and desorption processes of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. Fluorescence quenching provides a convenient assay to study the adsorption of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. When Fe3O4 particles (100 μg.mL−1) were added to the free FMN solution (500 nM), the fluorescence intensity of the FMN solution decreases rapidly and reached a minimum value of 200 a.u. within 30 min (blue, Fig. 6a). Fluorescence recovery was used to monitor the desorption process of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. The fluorescence intensity of the FMN@Fe3O4 suspension solution was restored immediately when PO4 3− (100 μM) was added. After a 50-min incubation time, the fluorescence signal intensity reached a maximum and maintained a relative stable value (red, Fig. 6a). Therefore, the incubation time of the FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe in PO4 3−-containing water was set at 60 min to ensure complete desorption in the subsequent experiments. The adsorption curve of FMN on the surface of Fe3O4 was further examined, as shown in Fig. 6b. Under a constant Fe3O4 concentration of 100 μg.mL−1, the amount of FMN added and the amount of FMN adsorbed show a linear relationship under a low concentration level (inset of Fig. 6b). A further increase in FMN concentration resulted in an overall Langmuir-type isotherm. The results indicate that FMN adsorption onto the surface of Fe3O4 is a monolayer adsorption process and can be reversed by the displacement of free PO4 3−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of PO4 3− determination
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity of PO4 3− determination using a FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe was firstly evaluated under optimized conditions (Fig. 7 ). A FMN@Fe3O4 suspension solution was incubated with various anions (10 μM) to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Only PO4 3− shows an obvious fluorescence restoration (Fig. 7a). Sulfite also shows a certain response value, which may have been a weak retardation of sulfite (SO3 2−), and does not imply that SO3 2− can bind to the surface of Fe3O4 (Frau et al., 2010). Considering that the content of SO3 2− is extremely low in a normal environmental water matrix, this interference can be avoided. We further evaluated the effect of other anions (10 μM) in the presence of 10 μM PO4 3− (Fig. 7b). There was no obvious effect on fluorescence intensity (inset of Fig. 7b). All the above demonstrates that the FMN@Fe3O4 sensing system has good selectivity for PO4 3− determination.

After the evaluation of selectivity of PO4 3− analysis using a FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe, the sensitivity of this sensing system in the analysis of PO4 3− was further investigated. Fluorescence analysis was carried out on an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The slit widths were set as 5.0 nm for both the excitation and emission monochromators. The adsorption peak at 445 nm was selected as excitation wavelength. The emission peak at 523 nm was selected to monitor the fluorescence recovery of FMN@Fe3O4 in response to different concentrations of PO4 3−. In the absence of PO4 3−, the fluorescence intensity of FMN@Fe3O4 suspension maintained a consistent value, indicating that the FMN molecules were stably adsorbed. In the presence of PO4 3−, the fluorescence intensity restored gradually and then remained at a constant level with a 60-min incubation time (Fig. 6a). As shown in Fig. 8a, high levels of PO4 3− can greatly restore the fluorescence of FMN@Fe3O4 suspensions. Fig. 8b plots this fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of PO4 3−. There was an excellent linear relation between the concentration of PO4 3− and the fluorescence intensity restored at low levels of PO4 3− (0.05 μM–0.75 μM, inset of Fig. 8b), which is highly desired in practical applications. The exact concentration of PO4 3− removal was further calculated through analysis the initial concentration and residual concentration of tested solutions using typical PMB-based spectrophotometry method. In the concentration range of working curve, the PO4 3− were removed absolutely through adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. The calibration coefficient fell between 0.995 and 0.999. The limit of quantification (LOQ) for this method was 50 nM (1.55 μg.L−1, calculated by P element), and the limit of detection (LOD) was 20 nM (0.62 μg.L−1, calculated by P element).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PO4 3− in real environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: No obvious signal of PO4 3− ion presence was detected in the tested tap water samples, while the concentration of PO4 3− ion in the lake water was measured at 1.64 ± 0.17 μM. Solutions of PO4 3− ion at 0.200, 0.400 and 0.600 μM dissolved in tap water and lake water, were used as quality-control samples. Accuracy and precision were further assessed by quintuplicate (n = 5) preparations of quality-control samples at three concentration levels. As listed in Table 1 , the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the spiked samples in tap water ranged from 3.3 % to 5.7 %, while the recovery ranged from 95.0 % to 105.7 %. RSD of the spiked samples in lake water ranged from 5.2 % to 8.8 % and the recovery ranged from 104.0 % to 107.3 %.

In order to evaluate the effectiveness of this PO4 3− analysis method in practical applications, typical municipal wastewater samples were collected from five individual sampling points (Influent; Aerobic tank; Anaerobic tank; Sedimentation tank; and Effluent) at a municipal sewage treatment plant located in Hefei, China. Prior to fluorescence analysis, all samples were centrifuged to remove mixed liquor suspended solids, filtered with a SPE cartridge to remove dissolved organic matters, and the high-concentration samples were further diluted ten times with ultrapure water. To determine the reproducibility of the experiments, five parallel analyses were conducted and the analytical results are listed in Table 2 . The concentration of PO4 3− contained in influent, aerobic tank, anaerobic tank, sedimentation tank, and effluent water were measured at 0.223 ± 0.003, 0.048 ± 0.004, 0.081 ± 0.004, 0.040 ± 0.002, and 0.035 ± 0.002 μM, respectively. All samples were also analyzed using the typical PMB-based spectrophotometry method, as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of PO4 3− detection using FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the experimental data above, this work will attempt to reveal the mechanism of PO4 3− detection using this FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe. The incubation of free FMN molecules with Fe3O4 particles results in an obvious fluorescence quenching, suggesting that FMN can be adsorbed onto the surface of Fe3O4 particles. Then, the addition of free PO4 3− restores the fluorescence of FMN (inset of Fig. 9a). Riboflavin (RF, known as VB2, an extracellular form of FMN), was selected and used as fluorophore under similar conditions. No obvious fluorescence quenching of the free RF solution occurred with the incubation of Fe3O4 particles; neither was there any fluorescence restoration after the addition of PO4 3− ions (Fig. 9a), which further confirmed the key role of the terminal phosphate group of FMN.

To further verify the interfacial interaction between FMN, RF and Fe3O4 particles, the zeta-potentials of Fe3O4 particles in aqueous solution were measured in the presence of different molecules or ions. As shown in Fig. 9b, zeta potentials of −38.2 ± 0.3 mV and − 47.5 ± 0.5 mV were observed for bare Fe3O4 particles and Fe3O4 particles in the presence of FMN, respectively. These results indicate that there exists an obvious interaction between FMN and Fe3O4 particles. Zeta potentials of Fe3O4 particles in the presence of PO4 3− ions shifted from−38.2 ± 0.3 mV to −57.7 ± 0.3 mV, which indicates an obvious interfacial interaction between PO4 3− ions and Fe3O4 particles. As reflected by zeta potentials analysis, the surface of Fe3O4 particles was negatively charged in this system, which was unfavorable for the electrostatic interaction between FMN/PO4 3− ions and Fe3O4 particles. It is well known that the electrostatic interaction is sensitive to the change of solvent ionic strength. However, the addition of electrolyte (NaCl) brings no obvious effect on the adsorption/desorption processes. Combined with FTIR analysis, zeta potentials analysis further confirmed that the formation of inner-sphere complexes rather than the electrostatic interaction between FMN/PO4 3− ions and Fe3O4 particles (Paltrinieri et al., 2017; Almasri et al., 2019). There was no marked variation in zeta potentials of Fe3O4 particles with the addition of RF molecules, which indicates that there has no obvious interfacial interaction between RF molecules and Fe3O4 particles. As the discussion above shows, the experimental results from zeta potential analysis are consistent with the fluorescence analysis results, which further confirm the key role of the terminal phosphate group of FMN molecule.

From the analysis above, an adsorption/desorption mechanism is proposed for the selective and sensitive detection of PO4 3− using this FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe (Fig. 9c). FMN is first adsorbed by its terminal phosphate group onto Fe3O4 particles to quench fluorescence (“Turn off”). In the subsequent step, free PO4 3− in aqueous samples replaces the adsorbed FMN and restores the quenched fluorescence (“Turn on”). Based on the variation in fluorescence intensity, selective and sensitive determination of PO4 3− is achieved using this FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescent probe, which is of great importance for real applications, such as environmental water-quality monitoring.

In summary, a novel FMN@Fe3O4 fluorescence system has been fabricated for selective and sensitive determination of PO4 3−, which shows excellent fluorescence response and good selectivity for PO4 3− detection. FMN, an intracellular form of vitamin B2, was used as fluorophore. An adsorption/desorption sensing mechanism is proposed after an in-depth analysis of the interfacial interaction between PO4 3− and FMN@Fe3O4. FMN is first adsorbed by its terminal phosphate groups onto Fe3O4 particles to quench fluorescence. Free PO4 3− replaces the adsorbed FMN and restores the quenched fluorescence to achieve the aim of PO4 3− detection. This strategy of competitive adsorption detection of PO4 3− was successfully validated in real water sample analysis. Additionally, all ingredients involved in this system are nontoxic, environmentally benign, and easily-available. Therefore, this method might be usefully employed in a broad range of real environmental applications, such as water-quality monitoring.
